Looking at the ruby library that the original issue was based on, it looks like they use the `verify` kwarg instead of `verify_signature` in the options dictionary at all.  

I can see taking it out of the dictionary completely to be the same as the ruby library or doing something like this:

```python
merged_options = self._merge_options(override_options=options)
if verify or merged_options.get('verify_signature'):
    self._verify_signature(payload, signing_input, header, signature,
                                       key, algorithms)

self._validate_claims(payload, options=merged_options, **kwargs)
```

This would avoid the `verify` kwarg from overriding the other options and require that the developer double opt-in to not verifying the signature.

This is an interesting issue - the necessity to let the threads run free is because TimerTasks have a call made to their cancel() method by the free() method in PIDController.
https://github.com/robotpy/robotpy-wpilib/blob/f489e60a373351904c13efba71e47e1606b74198/wpilib/wpilib/pidcontroller.py#L138-L144

If the thread isn't running at this point, this call will block on cancel()'s call to join(). But it won't necessarily be stopped on the next run through of the loop, so throwing an exception when it calls delay() would likely cause it to throw everytime.

For a thread that doesn't have a terminate mechanism, there is no method to call after the test terminates to stop it - so the reference to the object just gets dropped and the next test begins. If the thread doesn't have a way to stop and join it, I don't think there is much we can do.

Normally one-liners are to be avoided in Python, but I think in this case the code is too sparse and repetitive, so my suggestion would be:

```python
c1 = N/r1 if N % r1 == 0 else N/r1 + 1
c2 = N/r2 if N % r2 == 0 else N/r2 + 1
```

Alternatively,

```python
c1 = N/r1
if N % r1 == 0:
    c1 += 1
```

Either approach makes the symmetry between r1 and r2 more apparent.

Ah, I see that we're asking whether the user in the parameter has the given permission for the calling user object. However this is a little unexpected to me because Django's permissions are phrased differently. For instance, this LOC might be written as `facilityadmin.can_delete(member)`. This reads more like natural language -- "facility admin can delete member?" in contrast to `member.user_can_delete(facility_admin)` which is ambiguous IMO.

Alternatively we might write `facilityadmin.has_perm("can_delete", member)`, which additionally has the benefit of using the same method for checking all permissions (simply with different parameters) instead of requiring a separate method for every different type of permission.

Do you think it would be worthwhile to use either of these alternative phrasings? Or should we stick to what you've already written here?

It looks like your plan is to have multiple threads running for a thread-per-connection setup. While this will work it adds complexity and overhead. We could make this a single threaded application and do a reactor pattern. Essentially the for loop will be in the server. It will have the handle to the listener socket and each connected socket. Then all of the socket handles will be registered with an event dispatcher. When the event dispatcher returns then a handle to a socket is ready. If it is a connected socket then call process request. Otherwise create a client socket and register it with the event dispatcher.

See the reactor patter for more details.

Yep, defaulting to the global display name seems like a bad idea after all.

`self.api.get_display_name` doesn't raise anything and just returns `None` (which sure is unexpected according to the docstring of `User.get_display_name`), so here the try/except clause would just be replaced by `return room.members_displaynames.get(self.user_id)`.

However, it seems like `get_display_name` should indeed raise an exception, while if someone doesn't want that they should call `get_friendly_name` which ideally wraps around `get_display_name`, returning the user id if an exception was raised.

While we're at it, I don't think this is a good design. The spec doesn't say anything that suggests having two methods (to me it says that only `get_friendly_name` should exist, and should be called `get_display_name`). Also I can't see any use of having an exception raised in this kind of method.

So my proposal would be:
  - `User.get_display_name()` gives the global display name, or the user id if not set.
  - `User.get_display_name(room)` gives the display name corresponding to the room, or the user id if not set.
  - `User.get_friendly_name()` is deprecated and returns `User.get_display_name()`.

I agree they are mostly stylistic changes, thus the PR title minor cleanups.

The one change of substance is making parse options more DRY.  
I made this change intentionally because I have some very significant changes I am working on and it would simplify additions of new options.

Essentially without getting too much into it here I am changing to a configuration based system where the different styles are defined as a set of standard options instead of subclasses.

It would allow users to customize the import-order without necessarily requiring a new style to be created.  For example the PyCharm style recently implemented only covers a single way PyCharm can be configured and could have been implemented as `smarkets` with case-sensitivity option.

Sorry, can you be more specific about "comming last for `x-goog-api-client`? Currently the _CallSettings.merge() logic does not specify anything about ordering of `x-goog-api-client`.

As for `construct_settings merges` it merges static "config" data (not specific for request). The new method merges kwargs portion of the options, which is request-specific.

About ordering. Sorry, in my sample I put headers in "worng" ordering. The code itself actually merges in a way that `x-goog-api-client` header goes last. But in any case, the order of headers should not matter, as long as x-goog-api-client is unique in request (should be the case for us):
[RFC2616 secion-4.2](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-4.2):

> The order in which header fields with differing field names are received is not significant. However, it is "good practice" to send general-header fields first, followed by request-header or response-header fields, and ending with the entity-header fields.

    - If the user's `config` argument is a dictionary, use those options in addition to either the pylintrc file found at the directory of the file being linted, or the default one.
    - If the user's `config` argument is empty, use the pylintrc file found at the directory of the file being linted, or the default one.
2. Return a new `linter` object.

More specifically, do not ever try to re-use a linter object.

A huge struggle I have with Go in general is that it's basically impossible to tell the set of errors that can come out of a method call. I like pre-allocating errors so that I can document ahead-of-time exactly the sorts of errors that are allowed to come out of a particular package.

In this case, the stack trace capturing is not interesting, since these three errors are all coming from `PlanExecutor.Execute`. I'm most interested in being able to inspect these errors higher up the stack and avoid bad error messages like "an error occured while advancing the preview", which bubbles up to the top because we're using stringly-typed errors for control flow.
